subject: math
title: Building and Breaking Apart Numbers to 100,000
intro: Big numbers are made of smaller parts. In this lesson you will learn how to put number parts together (compose) and pull them apart (decompose) all the way up to 100,000.

vocabulary.meaning: A single number symbol from 0 to 9.
The value a digit has because of where it sits in a number.
To put number parts together to make one number.
To break a number apart into its place-value parts.
A way to write a number that shows the value of each digit added together.
The usual way we write a number, like 43,605.

body.text: What Is Place Value?
Every digit in a number has a place. The place tells you how much that digit is worth. The same digit can be worth a lot or a little, depending on where it sits.

body.alt: Place value chart showing the number 43,605 with digits in ten thousands, thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 520 120' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='5' y='5' width='510' height='110' fill='none' stroke='#333'/><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'><text x='55' y='30'>Ten Thousands</text><text x='155' y='30'>Thousands</text><text x='255' y='30'>Hundreds</text><text x='355' y='30'>Tens</text><text x='455' y='30'>Ones</text></g><g><line x1='105' y1='10' x2='105' y2='110' stroke='#999'/><line x1='205' y1='10' x2='205' y2='110' stroke='#999'/><line x1='305' y1='10' x2='305' y2='110' stroke='#999'/><line x1='405' y1='10' x2='405' y2='110' stroke='#999'/></g><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='34' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1a4'><text x='55' y='95'>4</text><text x='155' y='95'>3</text><text x='255' y='95'>6</text><text x='355' y='95'>0</text><text x='455' y='95'>5</text></g></svg>

body.text: Decompose: Break It Apart
To decompose 43,605, look at what each digit is worth and write the parts.

body.html:
43,605 = 40,000 + 3,000 + 600 + 0 + 5

The 0 in the tens place is a placeholder. It holds the spot so the other digits stay in the right place.

body.text: Compose: Put It Together
You can also go the other way. Add the parts to make the whole number.

body.title: Remember
body.text: Expanded form shows the value of each digit. Standard form is the short, normal way to write the number.

explanation: Line up each part by place value and add them: 80,000 + 3,000 + 800 + 80 + 7 = 83,887.

prompt: Which number is fifty-two thousand, seven?

options.rationale: Fifty-two thousand is 52,000, and seven ones is 7, so the number is 52,007.

explanation: The word 'seven' means 7 ones, so a zero holds the tens and hundreds places: 52,007.

options.rationale: Each digit is shown by its place value, and the tens place is 0 so it is left out.

explanation: The digits 4, 3, 6, 0, 5 are worth 40,000, 3,000, 600, 0, and 5. We can leave out the 0.

subject: math
title: The Ten-Times Rule: How Place Values Grow
intro: In our number system, each place is worth 10 times the place to its right. In this lesson you will see why a digit jumps in value every time it moves one spot to the left.

vocabulary.meaning: The value a digit has because of where it sits in a number.
Our number system, where each place is 10 times the one to its right.
Ten of something; 10 groups of the same amount.
The place all the way on the right; single units.
The place worth ten ones.
The place worth ten tens, or one hundred.

body.text: Each Place Is 10 Times Bigger
As you move left in a number, each place is worth 10 times the place just before it. Move right, and each place is worth one tenth as much.

body.alt: Arrows showing ones times ten equals tens, tens times ten equals hundreds, hundreds times ten equals thousands.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 540 110' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='16' text-anchor='middle'><rect x='10' y='40' width='90' height='40' fill='#eef' stroke='#336'/><text x='55' y='65'>Ones (1)</text><rect x='150' y='40' width='90' height='40' fill='#eef' stroke='#336'/><text x='195' y='65'>Tens (10)</text><rect x='290' y='40' width='110' height='40' fill='#eef' stroke='#336'/><text x='345' y='65'>Hundreds (100)</text><rect x='430' y='40' width='100' height='40' fill='#eef' stroke='#336'/><text x='480' y='65'>Thousands</text></g><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#a30'><text x='125' y='30'>x10</text><text x='265' y='30'>x10</text><text x='415' y='30'>x10</text></g><g stroke='#a30' fill='none'><path d='M100 50 q25 -20 50 0' marker-end='url(#a)'/><path d='M240 50 q25 -20 50 0' marker-end='url(#a)'/><path d='M400 50 q15 -20 30 0' marker-end='url(#a)'/></g><defs><marker id='a' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z' fill='#a30'/></marker></defs></svg>

body.text: Why It Works
It takes 10 ones to make 1 ten. It takes 10 tens to make 1 hundred. It takes 10 hundreds to make 1 thousand. The pattern keeps going!

body.html:
Look at the digit 5 in different places:

  • 5 in the ones place = 5
  • 5 in the tens place = 50 (10 times as big)
  • 5 in the hundreds place = 500 (10 times bigger again)

body.title: The Big Idea
body.text: Moving one place to the left makes a digit worth 10 times more. Moving one place to the right makes it worth one tenth as much.

prompt: The value of a digit in the hundreds place is how many times the value of the same digit in the tens place?

options.rationale: Each place is worth 10 times the place to its right, so hundreds are 10 times the tens.

explanation: The hundreds place is one spot to the left of the tens place, so it is 10 times larger.

prompt: What is the value of the 4 in 93,214?

options.rationale: The 4 is in the ones place, so its value is just 4.

explanation: In 93,214, the 4 sits in the ones place, so it is worth 4.

prompt: The 3 in 30 is worth 10 times the 3 in which number?

options.rationale: 3 ones times 10 equals 30, so the 3 in 30 is 10 times the 3 in 3.

explanation: The 3 in 30 is in the tens place, which is 10 times bigger than the ones place 3 in the number 3.

subject: math
title: Number Lines and Rounding to the Nearest 10 and 100
intro: A number line helps you see where a number lives. In this lesson you will name a number between two marks, and learn to round to the nearest 10 and the nearest 100.

vocabulary.meaning: A line with numbers marked in order from least to greatest.
A number you say when skip counting by tens, like 10, 20, 30.
To change a number to the nearest ten or hundred to make it simpler.
The closest one.
Exactly in the middle between two numbers, like 5 between 0 and 10.

body.text: Finding a Number on a Number Line
A number sits between two marks. To name it, count the small steps from the mark on the left.

body.alt: A number line from 40 to 50 with a dot on 47.

body.text: This dot is 7 steps past 40, so it shows 47. It is closer to 50 than to 40.
Rounding to the Nearest 10
To round to the nearest 10, find the two tens the number is between. Then pick the closer one. If the ones digit is 5 or more, round up. If it is 4 or less, round down.

body.html:
47 is between 40 and 50. The ones digit is 7, which is 5 or more, so round up to 50.

23 is between 20 and 30. The ones digit is 3, which is 4 or less, so round down to 20.

body.text: Rounding to the Nearest 100
To round to the nearest 100, look at the tens digit. If it is 5 or more, round up. If it is 4 or less, round down.

items: 472 rounds to 500 (the tens digit 7 is 5 or more, round up)
418 rounds to 400 (the tens digit 1 is 4 or less, round down)

body.title: Tip
body.text: 5 is the halfway number. When you round and the deciding digit is exactly 5, you round up.

prompt: A dot is 3 steps past 60 on a number line marked from 60 to 70. What number does it show?
explanation: Begin at 60 and count 3 steps forward to land on 63.

options.rationale: The ones digit is 7, which is 5 or more, so 47 rounds up to 50.

explanation: 47 is between 40 and 50. Since the ones digit 7 is 5 or more, round up to 50.

options.rationale: The tens digit is 1, which is 4 or less, so 418 rounds down to 400.

explanation: 418 is between 400 and 500. The tens digit 1 is 4 or less, so round down to 400.

subject: math
title: Comparing and Ordering Numbers Up to 100,000
intro: Which number is bigger? In this lesson you will compare whole numbers using the symbols <, >, and =, and put numbers in order from least to greatest.

vocabulary.meaning: To decide if a number is greater than, less than, or equal to another.
A symbol that points to the smaller number; the open side faces the bigger number.
A symbol meaning the first number is smaller than the second.
A symbol meaning two amounts are the same.
To line numbers up from least to greatest or greatest to least.
The value a digit has because of where it sits in a number.

body.text: Compare From the Left
To compare two numbers, start at the place on the far left. Compare those digits first. If they are the same, move one place to the right and compare again.

body.alt: A place value comparison of 32,450 and 32,510 with the hundreds digits highlighted.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 130' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g font-family='monospace' font-size='30' text-anchor='middle'><text x='240' y='45'>3 2 , 4 5 0</text><text x='240' y='95'>3 2 , 5 1 0</text></g><rect x='168' y='18' width='34' height='90' fill='none' stroke='#d33' stroke-width='2'/><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' fill='#d33'><text x='185' y='124'>4 < 5</text></g></svg>

body.text: Here the ten thousands (3) and thousands (2) match. The hundreds digits are different: 4 is less than 5. So 32,450 is less than 32,510.
Which Way Does the Symbol Point?
The open, wide side of the symbol always faces the bigger number. The point always points to the smaller number.

body.title: Ordering
body.text: To order numbers, compare them two at a time, then line them up from least to greatest.

options.rationale: The ten thousands and thousands match, but 4 hundreds is less than 5 hundreds, so 32,450 < 32,510.

explanation: Compare from the left. The first difference is in the hundreds place, where 4 is less than 5, so 32,450 < 32,510.

prompt: Which list is in order from least to greatest?

options.rationale: 8,750 is smallest, then 9,000, then 12,300, so this is least to greatest.

explanation: 8,750 is the smallest. 12,300 has more thousands than the others, so it is the largest. Least to greatest is 8,750; 9,000; 12,300.

options.rationale: 60,000 has a digit in the ten thousands place, so it is much greater than 6,000.

explanation: 60,000 reaches the ten thousands place while 6,000 only reaches the thousands place, so 60,000 is greater.

subject: math
title: Even or Odd? Pairing Up and Skip Counting
intro: Is a number even or odd? In this lesson you will pair objects into twos and skip count by 2 to decide whether a number up to 100 is even or odd.

vocabulary.meaning: A number that can be split into two equal groups with none left over.
A number that has one left over when you make pairs.
A group of two.
To count by a number other than one, like 2, 4, 6, 8.
The digit all the way on the right of a number.

body.text: Make Pairs to Check
To test a number, try to put the objects into pairs of two. If every object has a partner, the number is even. If one object is left alone, the number is odd.

body.alt: Eight dots arranged in four pairs with no leftover, showing 8 is even.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 130' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#36a'><circle cx='40' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='12'/><circle cx='110' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='110' cy='80' r='12'/><circle cx='180' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='180' cy='80' r='12'/><circle cx='250' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='250' cy='80' r='12'/></g><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='15' text-anchor='middle'><text x='145' y='120'>8 dots make 4 pairs — even!</text></g></svg>

body.text: The 8 dots form 4 full pairs with nothing left over, so 8 is even.

body.alt: Seven dots arranged in three pairs with one dot left over, showing 7 is odd.
body.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 130' role='img' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><g fill='#36a'><circle cx='40' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='40' cy='80' r='12'/><circle cx='110' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='110' cy='80' r='12'/><circle cx='180' cy='45' r='12'/><circle cx='180' cy='80' r='12'/></g><circle cx='250' cy='62' r='12' fill='#d33'/><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='15' text-anchor='middle'><text x='150' y='120'>7 dots — one left over, odd!</text></g></svg>

body.text: Use Skip Counting
Skip count by 2: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, ... Any number you land on is even. Numbers you skip over are odd.
A Quick Shortcut

body.html:
Look at the ones digit:

  • If it ends in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8, the number is even.
  • If it ends in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9, the number is odd.

body.title: Try It
body.text: 94 ends in 4, so it is even. 81 ends in 1, so it is odd. The shortcut works for any number.

options.rationale: 36 ends in 6, and 6 is even, so 36 splits into pairs with none left over.

explanation: 36 ends in 6. Numbers ending in 0, 2, 4, 6, or 8 are even, so 36 is even.

options.rationale: 47 ends in 7, and numbers ending in 7 are odd because one is always left over.

explanation: 47 ends in 7. Numbers ending in 1, 3, 5, 7, or 9 are odd, so 47 is the odd one.

prompt: When you skip count by 2 starting at 2, which number will you say?

options.rationale: Skip counting by 2 lands on even numbers, and 18 is even, so you say it.

explanation: Skip counting by 2 says only even numbers: 2, 4, 6, ... 18. So you will say 18.
